ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cooked escargot; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: land snail (Helix pomatia or Helix aspersa)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of escargot consists of 6 snails, yielding approximately 85g of cooked snail meat.

nutrient_source.title: Snails, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 15164); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for cooked escargot
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 85 kcal, protein 13.92g, fat 0.77g, carbohydrate 5.59g, sugar 0.0g, dietary fibre 0.0g, potassium 42mg, calcium 1053mg, iron 24.27mg, cholesterol 112mg) are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record for cooked escargot (cr_id F06080002, confidence 0.95). Sodium and saturated fat values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked snails (NDB 15164), which reports sodium 70mg and saturated fat 0.175g per 100g for plain cooked snail meat. These secondary fields are documented as adapted from the closest approved-source equivalent.

gi_source.title: Analogous estimate — high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate animal food; closest GI reference: protein-dominant foods with minimal digestible carbohydrate per University of Sydney GI Database methodology
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for cooked escargot in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Cooked escargot contains 5.59g total carbohydrate per 100g, predominantly glycogen, with 0g free sugar and 0g dietary fibre. High-protein, very-low-carbohydrate animal foods of this profile are consistently assigned GI values in the range of 0–10 in the University of Sydney GI Database methodology, as the carbohydrate content is too low to produce a meaningful glycaemic response. A conservative analogous estimate of GI 5 (low category) is assigned. Glycemic load per serving (85g) = (5 × 5.59 × 0.85) / 100 = 0.24, rounded to 0.3. This is a negligible glycaemic load. The estimate is clearly flagged as analogous and not a direct measurement.

hypertension.key_factor: Low intrinsic sodium (~70mg/100g) in plain cooked snail meat; risk escalates substantially with traditional butter-and-salt preparation
hypertension.guidance: Plain cooked escargot is low in sodium (~70mg/100g) and suitable for hypertension management, but patients should request preparation without added salt or butter, as restaurant-style escargot can deliver 300–500mg sodium per serving from added condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein (13.92g/100g) and very low carbohydrate (5.59g/100g) with 0g sugar; negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cooked escargot is an excellent low-glycaemic protein source for Type 2 Diabetes management, providing 13.92g protein and only 5.59g carbohydrate with 0g sugar per 100g; patients should avoid preparations with breadcrumb coatings or sugary sauces that add refined carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate cholesterol content (112mg/100g) and very low saturated fat (0.18g/100g) in plain form; traditional butter preparation significantly increases saturated fat and caloric density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain cooked escargot has very low saturated fat (0.18g/100g) and is acceptable in a heart-healthy diet in moderate portions, but the traditional garlic-herb butter preparation should be avoided or minimised by cardiovascular disease patients due to the substantial increase in saturated fat and total calories.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Exceptional calcium (1053mg/100g) and iron (24.27mg/100g) content critical for patients on corticosteroids facing bone loss and anaemia risk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cooked escargot provides exceptional calcium (1053mg/100g, approximately 105% of daily reference intake) and iron (24.27mg/100g), making it highly beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients on corticosteroids who face elevated bone loss and iron-deficiency anaemia risk; preparation with olive oil rather than butter is preferred to reduce pro-inflammatory saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cooked escargot refers to the edible meat of land snails (primarily Helix pomatia or Helix aspersa), cooked until firm and offering a chewy, dense texture with a mild, earthy, slightly briny flavour. The food is most closely associated with French haute cuisine, where it is classically prepared as Escargots de Bourgogne — snails baked in their shells with garlic-herb butter and parsley — though the nutritional data in this record reflects plain cooked snail meat without added butter or sauces. Per 100g, cooked escargot provides 85 kcal, 13.92g protein, 0.77g fat, and 5.59g carbohydrate with 0g sugar, making it a high-protein, low-calorie, low-fat food. Notably, it contains an exceptional 1053mg calcium per 100g and 24.27mg iron per 100g, values that are extraordinarily high for an animal protein source and are attributable in part to the shell-contact cooking method. Clinically, cooked escargot is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its negligible glycaemic impact (estimated GI 5, low category), suitable for hypertension when prepared without added salt or butter, and beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients on corticosteroids due to its outstanding calcium and iron content. Cardiovascular disease patients should note the moderate cholesterol content (112mg/100g) and limit traditional butter-based preparations. Escargot is classified as a mollusc and is a known allergen for shellfish-sensitive individuals.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95, reviewed status). Sodium and saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked snails, the closest approved-source equivalent. The exceptional calcium and iron values are consistent with published literature on shell-contact cooking of land snails and are accepted at the HPB-reported level. GI is an analogous estimate (low category, GI 5) clearly flagged as such.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — cooked plain snail meat contains no free sugars. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — snail meat is an animal product containing no dietary fibre. These are confirmed zero values, not missing data. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for cooked escargot are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this record, and the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for snails (NDB 15164) does not provide a complete micronutrient breakdown for all listed fields. Glycemic index is assigned as an analogous estimate of 5 (low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database methodology for high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate animal foods; no direct GI measurement exists for cooked escargot in any approved source. The calcium value of 1053mg/100g is exceptionally high and reflects shell-contact cooking; this value is accepted as reported by HPB (confidence 0.95) and is consistent with published data on land snail calcium content. Traditional escargot preparations with garlic-herb butter will substantially alter the sodium, saturated fat, and caloric profile from the values reported here, which reflect plain cooked snail meat only.
